origin: A short excerpt from the New Testament, specifically a Greek reading of the genealogy/lineage in the Gospel of Matthew (Matthew 1:16), presenting Jesus’ ancestry through Joseph (son of Jacob) as part of a broader birth/genealogical introduction.
author: Most likely the Evangelist Matthew (as the attributed author of the Gospel of Matthew), or a later scribe/translator preserving a Greek manuscript tradition of that passage (e.g., early NT manuscript copying).
thoughts: The author/scribe is likely aiming to establish Jesus’ legitimacy and messianic connection by grounding his identity in genealogy—especially linking Joseph (and thus Jesus’ social/legal line) to the patriarchal line. The motivation is theological and historical: to show that Jesus fulfills expectations associated with “the Christ.” The tone is matter-of-fact and documentary, not personal or emotional.
audience: Early Greek-speaking Christian communities (and, more broadly, educated readers familiar with Jewish genealogical tradition). The passage would be aimed at believers seeking confirmation of Jesus’ rightful status, as well as readers interested in scriptural fulfillment.
written_when: In the 1st century CE, likely around the late first century (commonly estimated ~70–90 CE for Matthew), though the exact date depends on manuscript and scholarly dating; certainly within the early Christian period before widespread second-century consolidation.
